D5 Design L3 Apply Reference

Chevreul's Circle & Contrast

the 1839 seventy-two-part circle, and the law it was built to teach — a colour casts its complement onto its neighbour

Chevreul, directing the Gobelins tapestry dye-works, found weavers' complaints about "weak" dyes were really a perceptual effect: every colour pushes its neighbour toward the complement. His 1839 circle of 72 hues was the tool for predicting it — the law of simultaneous contrast.

Pick a surround hue on the circle. The two centre squares are the same physical grey — yet on the coloured field your eye tints it toward the complement (marked opposite on the circle). That induced shift is Chevreul's law.
